
``code
{
	batch_id,
	user_id,
	window_start,
	window_end,
	record_count,
	
	avg_wpm,
	wpm_values,
	total_keystrokes,
	total_commands,
	total_errors,
	total_errors_fixed,
	total_commits,
	total_idle_seconds,
	top_files,
	language_distribution,
	code_snippets,
	total_copy_paste,
	
	fusion_result,
	thg_updates,
	
	status,
	created_at,
	processed_at
}

**`batch_id`** — A unique ID auto-generated by the Batch Processor when it picks up unprocessed raw records. Format: `BATCH-{YYYYMMDDHHMM}-{user_id[:8]}`. Used to trace which batch a raw record belonged to. After the batch runs, the raw records in `telemetry_raw` are stamped with this `batch_id` too.

**`user_id`** — The developer this batch belongs to. The Batch Processor groups all unprocessed raw records by `user_id` first, then creates one batch document per user per run.

**`window_start`** — The timestamp of the **earliest raw record** in this batch. i.e., when did the developer's activity in this batch actually begin.

**`window_end`** — The timestamp of the **latest raw record** in this batch. Together with `window_start`, it defines the time window this batch covers (e.g. 09:00 → 09:05 for a 5-minute batch run).

**`record_count`** — How many raw telemetry records were aggregated into this single batch. Tells you the density of activity — a high count = many 30-second pings = active developer.

---

### `aggregated_signals` — The Core Analytics Object

This is where all the raw 30-second snapshots get **summed and merged** into one meaningful window of developer activity.

**`avg_wpm`** — Average typing speed across the entire window. Calculated by taking all non-zero WPM values from the raw records and averaging them. Zero-WPM records (idle intervals) are excluded so idle time doesn't drag the average down unfairly.

**`wpm_values`** — The raw list of every non-zero WPM reading that went into `avg_wpm`. Kept separately so the Fusion Engine can do variance and trend analysis — e.g. was the developer consistently fast, or did they spike briefly?

**`total_keystrokes`** — Sum of all keystrokes across every raw record in the batch. Used as a raw signal of physical coding activity volume.

**`total_commands`** — Sum of all VS Code commands executed (format, save, run, refactor, etc.) across the batch window. High command count relative to keystrokes = developer is navigating/refactoring, not just typing.

**`total_errors`** — Sum of all `errors_encountered` values across raw records. Tells you how many times the developer hit a problem (lint errors, build failures, runtime crashes, etc.) in this window.

**`total_errors_fixed`** — Sum of all `errors_fixed` values. Paired with `total_errors` — the ratio of these two is a strong signal of debugging skill and problem resolution speed. `fixed / encountered = fix rate`.

**`total_commits`** — Total number of Git commits made across the batch window. A strong signal of delivery cadence and task completion rhythm.

**`total_idle_seconds`** — Total seconds the developer was inactive across the entire batch window. Used to distinguish _deep work_ (low idle) from _blocked/distracted_ sessions (high idle).

**`top_files`** — A merged map of every file the developer touched across all raw records, with the total time spent in each. Sorted by time descending, capped at 20. Tells the Fusion Engine and HR dashboard _exactly what the developer was working on_ during this window.

**`language_distribution`** — The `languages_used` maps from all raw records are merged together and then **normalized** so the values sum to 1.0. E.g. `{"python": 0.72, "yaml": 0.18, "json": 0.10}`. This tells the system what tech the developer was genuinely hands-on with during this window.

**`code_snippets`** — A list of up to 10 code samples collected from the raw records' `code_snippet` fields. These are fed into **CodeBERT** (the AI semantic analyzer in the Fusion Engine) to infer _what kind of code_ was being written — backend logic, frontend components, ML models, etc. Capped at 10 to keep memory use in check.

**`total_copy_paste`** — Sum of all `copy_paste_count` values. A supporting signal for the Fusion Engine — very high copy-paste relative to keystrokes can indicate the developer is integrating/adapting existing code rather than authoring from scratch, which affects skill confidence scoring.

---

**`fusion_result`** — Starts as `None`. After the Batch Processor sends `aggregated_signals` to the **Fusion Engine**, this gets populated with the full AI analysis result:

```json
{
  "status": "fusion_complete",
  "engine_version": "v2.0-top-tier",
  "reliability_check": { "is_reliable": true, "reliability_score": 0.94 },
  "skill_updates": {
    "backend":  { "strength": 0.82, "confidence": 0.91, "explanation": "..." },
    "frontend": { "strength": 0.45, "confidence": 0.78, "explanation": "..." },
    ...
  }
}
```

This is the computed **skill strength and confidence per domain** for this developer during this batch window.

**`thg_updates`** — Starts as `None`. After the Fusion Engine returns its result, the Batch Processor pushes each skill update to the **THG (Talent/Hiring Graph — Neo4j)**. This field is then set to `{ "count": N }` where N = the number of skills successfully written to the graph. It's an audit trail confirming how many skill nodes were updated in Neo4j as a result of this batch.

**`status`** — The processing state of this batch document. Lifecycle:

- `"pending"` — just created, Fusion hasn't run yet
- `"completed"` — Fusion Engine responded with `201`, skills pushed to THG
- `"failed"` — Fusion Engine call failed (error stored separately in `batch_doc["error"]`)

**`created_at`** — UTC timestamp of when this batch document was **created** by the Batch Processor. This is the server-side creation time, not the time of the raw telemetry data.

**`processed_at`** — UTC timestamp of when the Batch Processor **finished** with this document (Fusion + THG steps complete or failed). Always set before the document is inserted into MongoDB, so it's never truly `None` in the DB — but it starts as `None` in the builder before the processor sets it.
